name: Flight 93 Shot Down
description: The 'Flight 93 Shot Down' conspiracy theory alleges that United Airlines Flight 93, which crashed in Shanksville, Pennsylvania, on September 11, 2001, was intentionally shot down by the U.S. military to prevent it from reaching its intended target in Washington D.C. Proponents of this theory reject the official explanation that the passengers and crew heroically fought back against the hijackers, causing the plane to crash. The theory originated shortly after the 9/11 attacks, fueled by initial media reports that suggested debris was spread over a wider area than expected and ambiguous statements made by officials. Key evidence cited includes alleged witness testimonies claiming to have seen a missile strike the plane, discrepancies in the official crash site descriptions, and the perceived lack of identifiable aircraft wreckage. However, the mainstream perspective, supported by extensive forensic evidence, the 9/11 Commission Report, and the accounts of first responders, maintains that the plane crashed due to the actions of the hijackers and the passenger revolt. The wide debris field is attributed to the high-speed impact of the plane, and the lack of large wreckage is explained by the plane's disintegration upon impact. No credible evidence of a missile strike has been found, and the witness testimonies cited by proponents are often inconsistent or misinterpretations of events. Furthermore, the theory contradicts the cockpit voice recorder, which documented the struggle between passengers and hijackers. The theory persists due to distrust of the government, the emotional trauma of 9/11, and the desire for a simpler explanation than the complex reality of the events.
subtitle: Conspiracy theory surrounding the crash of Flight 93

claims.claim: Flight 93 was shot down by a U.S. military missile.
claims.evidence: Witness testimonies claiming to have seen a missile; widespread debris field.
claims.criticism: No credible physical evidence of a missile strike; debris field explained by high-speed impact; witness accounts are often unreliable or misinterpretations.

claims.claim: The official crash site description is inconsistent with the actual scene.
claims.evidence: Alleged discrepancies in the size and nature of the crash site; lack of identifiable aircraft wreckage.
claims.criticism: The crash site size is consistent with the high-speed impact and angle of descent; most wreckage was pulverized upon impact and buried underground.

claims.claim: The cockpit voice recorder is fabricated or manipulated.
claims.evidence: Skeptical interpretation of the available transcript and the lack of full unedited audio release.
claims.criticism: The cockpit voice recorder's authenticity has been verified by experts; the transcript aligns with other evidence and witness accounts; the full audio's release could endanger future investigations.

keyFigures.name: David Ray Griffin
keyFigures.role: Prominent 9/11 conspiracy theorist who promoted the 'shot down' theory.

keyFigures.name: Philip Shenon
keyFigures.role: Author of 'The Commission', a critical account of the 9/11 Commission.

mythologyConnections: Governmental deception (common motif in conspiracy narratives)

timeline.event: United Airlines Flight 93 crashes in Shanksville, Pennsylvania.
Initial media reports and ambiguous official statements contribute to the emergence of the 'shot down' theory.
Publication of the 9/11 Commission Report, which refutes the 'shot down' theory.
The 'shot down' theory continues to circulate online and in conspiracy theory circles.

sources.author: David Ray Griffin
sources.work: The New Pearl Harbor: Disturbing Questions About the Bush Administration and 9/11
sources.title: David Ray Griffin

sources.author: Lisa Beamer
